title: What concepts are required to deeply understand harness engineering?
note: User asked for the core concepts, papers, and ideas needed to deeply understand the article.
synthesis: To deeply understand the article, the user needs a layered map: model versus harness; harness as runtime/OS; context and memory engineering; workflow search; harness-as-code and executable search spaces; ACE/MCE/Meta-Harness; STOP/Self-Harness; AlphaEvolve/DGM; joint optimization with model weights; and the evaluator bottleneck. The deepest distinction is not memorizing paper names but knowing what object each system improves: context, workflow, solution, harness, improver, optimizer, or model weights.

interaction.content:
keep just helping me work through the material in the article.

what are the core concepts/papers/ideas I really need to understand to deeply understand this

The core map is: distinguish model/harness/deployment/workflow/memory/evaluator/optimizer; understand harnesses as OS-like runtime systems; track the progression from prompts to context to workflow to harness code to optimizer code; learn ACE, MCE, Meta-Harness, STOP, Self-Harness, AlphaEvolve, DGM, and joint optimization by asking what each one improves; and treat evaluation as the central bottleneck.

title: What are the biggest implications of harness engineering?
note: User asked for the main things and biggest implications from the article.
synthesis: The article implies that the harness may be the most practical near-term substrate for recursive self-improvement: the editable runtime around the model can improve capability without changing weights. The user's The AGI Post Ops repo is a concrete example of a publication harness: it owns operating rules, skills, project workflows, tasks/RDs, agent tools, dashboard state, graph memory, and safety boundaries around the separate product/publication app. The implication is that improving this ops layer could improve future publication work and future ops-layer improvement.

interaction.content: what are the main things/biggest implications from the artilcle?

The biggest implications are: harnesses may be the path to near-term RSI; capability can improve without changing model weights; prompts are only the surface layer; memory should live outside context; evals become the bottleneck; code makes harnesses searchable; and humans still need to supply taste, goals, and oversight.

dont focus on learning lab as the example > think about something like this as the example - which is the operating system behind my publication:

[bradwmorris/theagipost_ops](https://github.com/bradwmorris/theagipost_ops)

Using The AGI Post Ops is a better example. It is not the publication model or the public product app; it is the operating harness around publication work: rules, skills, project workflows, tasks/RDs, agent tools, dashboard state, graph memory, safety boundaries, and routing between feature/publication/ops work.

title: Why are fuzzy evaluators the bottleneck for self-improving harnesses?
note: User noticed that unit tests are unusually crisp, while summaries, idea extraction, and ranking depend on judgment or taste.
synthesis: The central bottleneck is that many valuable tasks do not have unit-test-shaped feedback. For summaries, research taste, idea ranking, and learning artifacts, the evaluator must judge whether the output captured the real crux, prioritized the right ideas, and will remain useful later. If that verifier has weak taste, the self-improvement loop can optimize for shallow proxies instead of real value.

interaction.content:
im not an expert software eng, but i undersand how some thing, like unit tests for example, might indicate whether something is right/wrong - but most things aren't this shape > ie,

let's think of some examples here?

- does the summary capture the crux of the argument?
- were the ideas extracted and ranked correctly?

so this all just depends on whether or not the final verifier has good taste to understand if the output is good/bad?

Yes, that is the evaluator bottleneck. Unit tests give crisp feedback, but many research and learning outputs require judgment: did the summary catch the real crux, did the ranking reflect importance, and will the artifact be useful later? In those cases, the loop is only as good as the verifier's taste or the proxy signals it uses.

title: Why might the harness be the path to RSI?
marker: Final synthesis grill / crux of article
note: User sharpened the article's TLDR from harnesses being useful to harnesses potentially being the tractable path to recursive self-improvement.
synthesis: The article's sharper crux is that harness engineering may be the practical near-term substrate for recursive self-improvement. The harness includes editable parts around the model: tools, scripts, workflows, evals, memory, prompts, permissions, and logs. The RSI implication appears only when those editable parts are inside a loop: run the agent, observe failures, propose harness changes, evaluate them, keep the better harness, then use that improved harness for future improvement.

interaction.content: are we sure this is correct > isn't the whole thing being implied here that > the harness may be the path to rsi?

Yes. The sharper crux is that harness engineering may be the practical near-term path to RSI: the harness is not just support infrastructure; it may be the editable substrate that lets a model improve the system that helps it improve later.

Yes: this is where the loop matters. A harness part changing once is just engineering. RSI-shaped harness improvement means the system can use outcomes from its own work to change the harness, evaluate the change, keep it, and thereby become better at the next improvement cycle.

title: What exactly is a harness in a coding agent?
note: User began the review by defining a harness broadly as the structure/system surrounding the model.
synthesis: A harness is the runtime machinery that makes a model act usefully on a task: prompt assembly, context retrieval, tool access, memory, workflow loops, permission checks, logging, retries, and evaluation. The user's first answer had the right center but was too broad, because deployment infrastructure and product UI can also be called surrounding systems.

That answer has the right center, but it is too broad: "system surrounding the model" could also describe deployment infrastructure or product UI. Sharpen it using a coding agent: name three concrete responsibilities that belong inside the harness, and one surrounding thing that is not the harness.

title: Why is full RSI still hard?
note: User asked for a simple explanation of the bottlenecks section.
synthesis: Weng's simple point is that self-improvement loops work best when success is easy to measure, but real research and real software work are hard to measure. The main bottlenecks are fuzzy evaluators, growing memory, poor handling of negative results, diversity collapse, reward hacking, short-term optimization, and the need for human oversight. These are all ways the loop can look like it is improving while actually becoming narrower, brittle, or misaligned with long-term value.

interaction.content:
explain this simple

Toward full RSI, researchers have made real progress, but several bottlenecks remain...

The simple version: current self-improvement works best when the system has a clear scoreboard. Coding tests pass or fail. A kernel is faster or slower. A benchmark score goes up or down. But real research and real engineering often do not have a clean scoreboard. Weak evaluators mean we cannot easily tell if an idea is actually good. Context and memory means long projects need durable memory, not just a giant prompt. Negative results means agents must learn from failures, not only chase successes. Diversity collapse means optimization may produce many copies of the same decent idea. Reward hacking means the agent may game the metric instead of solving the real problem. Long-term success means short-term task wins may damage maintainability or future work. Humans still matter because judgment, taste, safety, and long-term direction are not fully captured by current metrics. Check-back: which bottleneck is the root of many others: bad memory, or bad measurement/evaluation?

title: What is joint optimization with model weights?
marker: Section: Joint Optimization with Model Weights
note: User asked what the section title means after studying harness and evolutionary search.
synthesis: Joint optimization with model weights means improving two layers in the same loop: the harness around the model and the model parameters themselves. Earlier examples usually keep the base model fixed and change prompts, memory, workflow, tools, or harness code. Joint optimization asks whether the system should sometimes update the harness and sometimes train or adapt the model weights, based on feedback from recent task trajectories.

interaction.content: Joint Optimization with Model Weights - what is?

This is the section where Weng says: until now, most examples improved the non-parametric layer around the model, meaning the harness. Joint optimization means the loop can improve both the harness and the model weights. Harness update means changing external machinery: prompts, tools, memory, workflow, permissions, evaluators. Weight update means changing the model itself through training, fine-tuning, continual learning, or test-time adaptation. The SIA example has a feedback agent that looks at recent trajectories and chooses which kind of update is needed next: change the harness, or update the model weights. Check-back: if the agent fails because it used the wrong workflow, which layer should change? If it fails because the model lacks the skill even with the right workflow, which layer should change?

title: What is the Darwin Godel Machine changing?
note: User paused on the Darwin Godel Machine paragraph and asked what it is.
synthesis: Darwin Godel Machine is an evolutionary self-improvement setup where the object being evolved is the coding agent's own harness-code repository. This is different from AlphaEvolve-style solution improvement: AlphaEvolve mainly evolves candidate programs or algorithms, while DGM evolves versions of the agent/harness that writes and edits code. A parent agent reads its own benchmark logs, proposes harness-code changes, creates a child agent, and the child is evaluated; good child agents are kept in the population.

interaction.content:
what is this?

Unlike the methods above, which focus on solution improvement, Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM; [Zhang et al. 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.22954)) e

DGM is the more direct self-improving-agent version of evolutionary search. AlphaEvolve asks: can we evolve better solution programs? DGM asks: can we evolve better versions of the coding agent/harness itself? The loop is: start with one coding agent, run it on benchmarks, inspect failure logs, let that agent edit its own harness code, create a child agent, evaluate the child, and keep it if it performs well. The model weights stay fixed; the surrounding agent code changes. Check-back: compared with AlphaEvolve, is DGM evolving the code solution to a task, or the agent/harness that solves tasks?

title: What does evolutionary search mean for improving a harness?
note: User paused at the Evolutionary Search section and wanted a grounded explanation.
synthesis: Evolutionary search means treating harness designs as candidate programs: generate variations, evaluate them with tests or benchmarks, keep better ones, and use those as parents for the next round. In this article, it is the same basic idea as natural selection, but applied to code, workflows, prompts, memory rules, and optimizer logic.

interaction.content:
help me really udnerstand this section

Evolutionary Search

Evolutionary search here means selection over executable designs. You do not just ask the model for one best harness. You keep a population of candidates, mutate or recombine them, evaluate each one, keep the ones that score well, and use those as parents for the next generation. In this section, Weng is saying that because harnesses can be represented as code, they become searchable objects. A candidate might differ in prompt wording, workflow order, retry policy, memory format, tool-use logic, or evaluator logic. The evolutionary loop is: candidate design -> run it -> score it -> keep/reject -> generate variants. The important part is the evaluator. Without an external score, it is just brainstorming. With a score, the system can search. AlphaEvolve is the clean example: candidate programs are mutated by LLMs, tested by an evaluator, and successful variants stay in the pool. The same principle can apply to harnesses: evolve the system around the model, not necessarily the model weights. Check-back: if a harness variant survives into the next round, what made it survive: the model liked the idea, or evaluation showed it worked better?

title: How does AlphaEvolve work?
note: User asked to be taken through AlphaEvolve in the evolutionary search section.
synthesis: AlphaEvolve is best understood as an evolutionary program-search system. It uses LLMs/agents inside a harness-like orchestration loop, but it is not primarily a coding-agent product like Codex or Claude Code, and it is not primarily evolving its own harness. The thing being evolved is usually candidate code/programs/algorithms. The system selects parent programs, asks frozen LLMs to propose code diffs, evaluates the child programs, and keeps useful variants. Weng uses it as evidence that executable code spaces can be searched and improved, which is why similar logic can later be applied to harnesses.

interaction.content: take me through the whole alphaevolve thing

AlphaEvolve is evolutionary search over code. Start with candidate programs in a pool. Pick a parent program. Give an LLM the parent code, results so far, instructions, and sometimes meta information. The LLM proposes a code diff, a small mutation/improvement. Run the child program. Score it with an evaluator, such as speed, correctness, benchmark score, or scientific objective. If it improves or is useful, keep it in the pool. Repeat. Over time the pool evolves toward better programs. The important details in Weng's summary: the LLM is frozen, so the model weights are not changing; the system improves by changing candidate programs. The agent may see the full repo, but only certain marked regions may be meant for evolution, such as `EVOLVE-BLOCK-START` / `EVOLVE-BLOCK-END`. The meta-prompt can also evolve, meaning the instructions for generating future mutations can improve too. How it fits: AlphaEvolve is not primarily harness self-improvement; it is solution/program evolution. Weng uses it to show why evolutionary search works well when candidates are executable and easy to score. Check-back: in AlphaEvolve, what is evolving: the LLM's weights, or the pool of candidate programs and prompts used to mutate them?
is AlphaEvolve specifically a coding harness? or agent? or what?

AlphaEvolve is not best categorized as a coding harness or a normal coding agent. It is an evolutionary program-search system. It uses LLM agents as proposal engines, and it has a harness-like orchestration system around them, but the main object being optimized is candidate code/programs/algorithms. So the clean taxonomy is: base LLM = proposes mutations; agent/scaffold = manages proposal, editing, and evaluation steps; AlphaEvolve system = evolutionary search loop over executable programs. Weng brings it up because it shows a general recipe: if something can be represented as code and evaluated, an LLM-driven search loop can improve it. Harnesses are one possible future target of that recipe, but AlphaEvolve itself is mostly about evolving programs. Check-back: in your own words, is AlphaEvolve the thing being evolved, or the system doing the evolving?

title: What is Self-Harness and how does it fit in?
note: User asked what Self-Harness is and how it fits into the self-improving harness discussion.
synthesis: Self-Harness is a more direct version of the self-improving harness idea: an LLM agent proposes changes to its own harness, evaluates whether those changes improve performance, and accepts only useful changes. It fits after STOP because STOP improves the improver/scaffold in an abstract sense, while Self-Harness specifically targets the harness surrounding the agent. It also relates to ADAS, AFlow, and Meta-Harness because all treat agent design as something editable, executable, and evaluable.

interaction.content:
A more recent work, Self-Harness ([Zhang et al. 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09498)), relies on LLM agents to improve their own harness via a propose-evaluate-accept loop.

back to this? what is this? how does it fit in?

Self-Harness is the idea made very direct: let the agent improve the wrapper around itself. The loop is: propose a harness change, evaluate it on tasks, accept it only if it helps. Harness change could mean changing prompts, memory rules, context retrieval, tool-use logic, workflow steps, validation, retries, or other scaffold code. How it fits: STOP says 'improve the improver,' but more abstractly. ADAS/AFlow search over agent workflows. Meta-Harness searches over harness code. Self-Harness says: the agent itself participates in improving its own harness through propose -> evaluate -> accept. The key thing is the accept step. It prevents every random self-change from being kept. The system needs an evaluator: did this new harness actually perform better? Grounding analogy: you do not just rewrite your study routine and assume it is better. You try the new routine for a few tasks, compare results, and keep it only if it improves learning. Check-back: what makes Self-Harness safer than just letting an agent rewrite itself freely: the propose step, or the evaluate-and-accept gate?

title: How can prompts be part of code if LLM outputs are nondeterministic?
marker: Self-Improving Harness / code as universal language follow-up
note: User asked how prompts fit into code as a universal harness language when prompt outputs are nondeterministic, unlike ordinary deterministic code/tool behavior.
synthesis: Code does not make the model's output deterministic. Instead, code acts as the container and control system around nondeterministic model calls. It can store prompt templates, choose variables, retrieve context, set model parameters, validate outputs, retry failures, route tool calls, log results, and run evaluations. A prompt is often a string or template inside the program; the LLM call is a probabilistic operation inside an otherwise executable workflow. This is why harness designs can be represented as code even when one component, the model, is nondeterministic.

interaction.content:
back to this:

So when she says code is a universal language, she means many different harness designs can be expressed as executable programs.

I just don't fully understand this bit. What does it mean that the prompt and the tool, I understand how tools, for example, are expressed as programs or code, but I don't understand how there are other things in there, like the prompts, which they can fit inside the code, but they're not deterministic in the same way, like the output.

You're exactly right to notice the difference. The prompt is not deterministic in the same way a normal function is. The point is not that code turns the model into deterministic software. The point is that code can contain and control the nondeterministic parts. A harness program might say: choose this prompt template, fill in these variables, retrieve these files, call the model with temperature 0.2, require JSON output, validate the JSON, retry if invalid, run this tool if requested, score the final answer. The model call is probabilistic, but the surrounding procedure is executable. So the prompt is part of code as a template/string/config, while the model response is an uncertain result produced inside that programmed workflow. Analogy: a weather simulation program may include randomness, but it is still code because the simulation rules, inputs, outputs, and evaluation are specified. Check-back: is the key claim that prompts become deterministic code, or that prompts can be embedded inside a coded control loop that manages the model's nondeterministic outputs?

title: What is STOP improving: the solution or the improver?
note: User was stuck on STOP notation: seed improver, utility function, black-box model, improved solution, meta-utility, and recursive improver updates.
synthesis: STOP is about improving the improver, not just improving one answer. An improver is a procedure that takes a candidate solution, a scoring function, and a language model, then returns a better solution. STOP evaluates an improver across many downstream tasks using meta-utility: the average quality of solutions it produces. Then it asks the model to create a better version of the improver itself. This is recursive scaffolding improvement: use the current improver to improve the mechanism that improves future solutions.

interaction.content:
i don't understand this:

Self-Taught Optimizer (STOP; [Zelikman et al. 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.02304)) is one of the early examples of recursive scaffolding improvement. A seed improver at step takes an initial solution, a utility function, and a black-box language model, and returns an improved solution. The goal of STOP is not directly to improve the solution but to improve the improver itself. First, let’s define the meta-utility as the average utility of a given improver function over a collection of downstream tasks. Because improving the improver function is an optimization problem itself, we can recursively get a new version of the improver based on its performance measured by meta-utility via a self-improvement update.

Ignore the math first. STOP is saying: don't just use the model to improve one answer; use the model to improve the procedure that improves answers. Imagine you have a helper script called `Improve(solution)`. You give it a rough answer, and it returns a better answer. Normal optimization asks: did the answer get better? STOP asks: can we make `Improve` itself better, so future answers improve more reliably? The pieces are: initial solution = rough draft; utility function = scoring rule; black-box model = the LLM; improver = the method/scaffold that uses the model to improve the draft; improved solution = better draft; meta-utility = average score of the improver across many tasks. Then the recursive step is: use the current improver plus its performance results to propose a better improver. Simple analogy: instead of only improving one essay, you improve your editing checklist. Then that better checklist improves future essays. Check-back: in STOP, is the main target the individual solution, or the method that improves solutions?

title: Why does Weng emphasize code as a universal language?
marker: Self-Improving Harness / code as universal language
note: User asked why Weng writes code with sparkles in the Self-Improving Harness section.
synthesis: Weng emphasizes code because code can express the whole harness, not just prompts: prompts, tool calls, subagents, memory, workflow logic, permissions, evaluation, retries, and control flow. If the harness is code, then a coding agent can edit it, run it, test it, and improve it. The sparkles are playful emphasis: code is the common representation that lets systems like Meta-Harness, ADAS, and AFlow turn agent design into something executable and searchable.

interaction.content:
up to this > Self-Improving Harness

why she say code in sparkles?

. As we have seen in work like Meta-Harness, ADAS, and AFlow, ✨code✨ is a universal language for

The sparkles are playful emphasis, but the point is serious: code is the representation that can describe the whole agent system. A prompt can describe behavior, but code can implement behavior. It can define prompts, choose context, call tools, run subagents, store memory, check permissions, run evals, retry failures, and route control flow. So when she says code is a universal language, she means many different harness designs can be expressed as executable programs. That matters because once the harness is code, a coding agent can modify it and test whether the modification works. Meta-Harness edits harness code. ADAS writes new agent workflows in code. AFlow represents workflows in code/graphs and searches over them. Grounding: prompt = advice to the model; code = the machine that actually controls the model's environment. Check-back: why is code a better target for self-improvement than only a prompt: because it is prettier, or because it can be executed, tested, and changed?

title: What is AFlow in simple terms?
note: User asked for a simpler explanation of AFlow in the workflow design section.
synthesis: AFlow is a method for automatically improving agent workflows. It represents a workflow as a graph: nodes are LLM calls or agent actions, and edges decide what happens next. Instead of manually choosing the best workflow, AFlow starts from a template, asks an LLM to propose modified workflows, runs and scores them, keeps improved ones, and searches further from the better candidates. In simple terms, it is automated trial-and-error over workflow diagrams.

interaction.content: AFlow - explain this in more simple terms

AFlow is basically a way to automatically search for a better agent workflow. Think of a workflow as a flowchart. Each node is something the agent does, like ask the model to reason, call a tool, critique an answer, revise, or produce the final output. Each edge says what happens next. AFlow starts with a simple workflow, like a template. Then it asks an LLM to make a variation of that workflow. It runs the new workflow on tasks, scores it, and keeps it if it improves. Then it repeats. The MCTS part just means it does this search in a smarter tree-like way: explore some new branches, exploit branches that already look promising. Simple analogy: instead of manually designing the perfect recipe, AFlow keeps trying recipe variants, tasting/scoring them, and exploring the best branches. Check-back: is AFlow mainly changing the model weights, or changing the sequence/graph of steps the agent follows?

title: Is ADAS a real system or just a concept?
note: User asked whether Automated Design of Agentic Systems is a real system being explained in the article.
synthesis: ADAS is a real research framework and implemented algorithm, not just a concept. Hu, Lu, and Clune propose Meta Agent Search: start with an archive of simple agent workflows, have a meta-agent write new agent workflow code inspired by prior designs, evaluate candidates, and add successful ones back to the archive. It is a research prototype for automatically discovering better agent designs, not necessarily a deployed product.

interaction.content:
is this a real system being explained here?

 Automated Design of Agentic Systems (ADAS; [Hu et al. 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.08435)) formulates agent design itself as an optimization problem, “meta-agent search” where a meta-agent proposes new designs of agentic workflows.

Initialize an archive of agentic workflows with simple agents such as CoT and self-refine.
Ask a meta-agent to program new agents, all in code, inspired by existing solutions in the archive.
The meta-agent first generates a high-level description of the new workflow, and then implements it in code.
The draft program then goes through two self-refine steps (i.e. ask the model to provide feedback and then ask the same model to refine the previously generated outputs based on the feedback; [Madaan et al. 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17651)) by the meta-agent to check its novelty.
Evaluate each new candidate and add successful ones back to the archive.
Repeat steps 2-3 until the maximum iteration count is reached.

Yes. ADAS is a real research framework/paper, and the authors demonstrate an implemented algorithm called Meta Agent Search. It is not just a metaphor. But treat it as a research prototype, not necessarily a polished product people are using day-to-day. What it does: start with simple agent designs, like Chain-of-Thought or self-refine; keep them in an archive; ask a meta-agent to invent a new agent workflow, described in plain language and then implemented in code; run self-refinement on the draft; evaluate the new agent; if it works well, add it to the archive; repeat. The key idea is recursive-ish: an agent is designing better agents. Not by changing model weights, but by changing the workflow/scaffold around the model. Check-back: in ADAS, what is being improved: the base model weights, or the code/workflow that wraps the model?

title: Why is workflow design a search problem?
marker: Workflow Design / workflow as search problem
note: User asked what it means that the design space for workflow is enormous and workflow design can be thought of as a search problem.
synthesis: Workflow design is a search problem because there are many possible ways to arrange an agent's steps, roles, tools, checks, retries, and stopping rules. Instead of hand-picking one workflow, a system can generate candidate workflows, run them on tasks, score their performance, and keep or refine the better ones. The search space is the set of possible workflows; the evaluation score tells the system which designs work better.

interaction.content:
i don't fully understand this

The design space for workflow is enormous, and naturally we can think of workflow design as a search problem -

This means there are many possible workflows an agent could follow, and we do not know in advance which one is best. Example: for a coding task, possible workflows include: answer immediately; plan first then code; run tests before editing; write code then run tests; ask a critic agent to review; spawn subagents to inspect different files; retry twice after failure; stop only after passing tests. Each choice changes the workflow. The number of combinations explodes. That is the 'design space.' Calling it a search problem means: instead of a human manually choosing one workflow, we can generate many candidate workflows, run them on benchmark tasks, score them, and keep the ones that work better. So: workflow design space = all possible process recipes; search = trying candidates and selecting better ones. Check-back: if we compare 20 different agent workflows on the same task set and keep the highest-scoring one, what are we searching over: model weights, prompts only, or workflow designs?

title: Are workflow design examples just copies of Karpathy autoresearch?
note: User asked whether the Workflow Design examples are basically copies of Karpathy's autoresearch to create self-improving research systems.
synthesis: The workflow design examples are not simply copies of Karpathy's autoresearch, but they share the same core idea: make research or agent work into an explicit loop or pipeline that can be run, evaluated, and improved. AI Scientist builds a research-paper pipeline; ScientistOne emphasizes evidence and verifiability; Autodata uses challenger/solver/verifier roles to generate useful data; ADAS searches over agent workflow designs; AFlow represents workflows as graphs and optimizes them. The progression moves from handcrafted research workflows toward automated search over workflow designs.

interaction.content:
im in the workflow design section >

help me understand through this>

are all these examples basically examples which copied karpathy's auto-research to.create some self improving research system? explain those to me

Not exactly copied from Karpathy, but yes, they are in the same family. Karpathy's autoresearch is a clean minimal example of the pattern: turn research into a loop where the agent proposes, executes, observes results, improves, and repeats. The Workflow Design section is showing variations of that broader idea. AI Scientist: a handcrafted research pipeline: generate idea -> write code -> run experiments -> analyze results -> write paper -> review. ScientistOne: similar research automation, but with verifiability as the main constraint: every claim must trace back to evidence. Autodata: not full paper-writing; it is a workflow for generating training/eval data using roles like challenger, weak solver, strong solver, and verifier. ADAS: instead of hand-designing one workflow, a meta-agent designs new agent workflows in code, evaluates them, and keeps successful ones. AFlow: represents workflows as graphs and uses search to improve the graph. So the progression is: handcrafted auto-research workflow -> evidence-constrained workflow -> data-generation workflow -> automated search over workflow designs. Check-back: which examples are manually designed workflows, and which ones are trying to search for better workflows automatically?

title: How can a harness be written as code?
note: User asked how a model harness can be written as code.
synthesis: A harness is code because it is the software wrapper that decides how the model is called and what surrounds it: prompt assembly, context retrieval, memory writes, tool definitions, tool-call execution, workflow loops, permission checks, retries, logging, evaluation, and result formatting. The model weights are not the harness; the harness is the program that orchestrates the model's interaction with tools, state, and tasks.

interaction.content: how can the harness be written as code?

Because the harness is the software wrapper around the model. The model is called by code, and that code decides what happens before, during, and after the model call. For example, harness code can do: build the prompt; retrieve relevant memories/files; define available tools; execute tool calls; decide the workflow loop; check permissions; run tests/evals; save logs; retry after failure; summarize the final answer. Tiny example: `context = retrieve(task); prompt = buildPrompt(task, context); answer = callModel(prompt, tools); if answer.callsTool: runTool(answer.toolCall); score = evaluate(answer)`. None of that is the model weights. It is ordinary software that orchestrates the model. So when Weng says harness design can become executable, she means these choices can live in editable code rather than just human-written advice. Check-back: in Codex, is `GPT-5` the harness, or are things like tool definitions, file access, shell execution, approval rules, and artifact saving the harness code around the model?

title: What does it mean for harness design to become an executable search space?
note: User asked what Weng means by saying that once harness design becomes an executable search space, a strong coding agent can exploit the same design space human engineers use.
synthesis: The claim is that if harness design choices are represented as editable, runnable code, then an AI coding agent can search over those choices the way a human engineer would: change retrieval logic, memory format, prompt assembly, tool policies, workflow loops, evals, and retry behavior; run the harness; score it; keep improvements. The design space is 'executable' because each candidate harness can actually be run and evaluated, not just discussed as an idea.

interaction.content:
i don't fully understand this >

Still, the important lesson is clear: once harness design becomes an executable search space, a strong coding agent can exploit the same design space human engineers use.

She means: if the harness is written as code that can be edited, run, tested, and scored, then improving the harness becomes a search problem a coding agent can work on. Human engineers improve a harness by trying design changes: change how context is retrieved, change how memory is stored, change the workflow loop, change tool permissions, change evals, change retries, run experiments, compare results. Once those choices live in executable code, a strong coding agent can do the same thing: propose a change, edit the harness, run it on benchmark tasks, inspect failures, keep or discard the change. 'Executable search space' = a set of possible harness designs that can actually be run and evaluated. 'Same design space human engineers use' = prompts, retrieval, memory, tools, workflows, evals, code structure, policies, and logging. Concrete example: instead of asking an agent 'what would be a better memory system?', you let it edit the memory module, run 100 tasks, measure accuracy/token cost/failures, and keep the version that works better. Check-back: why does it matter that the design space is executable rather than just described in prose?

title: How are ACE, MCE, and Meta-Harness different?
marker: Context Engineering / ACE vs MCE vs Meta-Harness
note: User asked whether ACE, MCE, and arXiv:2603.28052 are different things.
synthesis: Yes, they are related but different levels. ACE optimizes the context content: it maintains a structured playbook of useful context items. MCE optimizes the method for constructing context: it evolves skills/recipes for finding, selecting, filtering, and formatting context. Meta-Harness goes broader and optimizes harness code itself: the source code that decides what to store, retrieve, and present to the model. The progression is context content, context-building recipe, then broader harness code.

interaction.content:
is this a different thing again?

so there's ace, mce and https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.28052

Yes. They are three related ideas at different levels. ACE: optimize the context itself. It keeps a cleaner playbook of useful lessons/bullets, instead of dumping everything into the prompt. MCE: optimize the recipe for building context. It asks: what skill/process should we use to search, select, filter, format, and update context? Meta-Harness: optimize the harness code more broadly. The arXiv paper is Meta-Harness: End-to-End Optimization of Model Harnesses. Its target is not just context notes or context recipes; it searches over the code that determines what information to store, retrieve, and present to the model. So the ladder is: ACE = better context memory; MCE = better method for making context memory; Meta-Harness = better harness code around the model, including context management. Check-back: which one is broadest: ACE, MCE, or Meta-Harness?

title: What is Meta Context Engineering doing in plain language?
marker: Context Engineering / Meta Context Engineering notation
note: User was stuck on the MCE notation: skills, context functions, static components, dynamic operators, bi-level optimization, skill database, crossover, and rollout feedback.
synthesis: MCE separates two things: the context itself and the method for building context. A skill is a recipe for constructing useful context for a task. Static components are reusable resources like prompts, knowledge bases, and code libraries. Dynamic operators are actions like search, selection, filtering, and formatting. The inner loop tries to build the best context for training tasks using a given skill. The outer loop chooses or evolves the best skill by checking validation performance. A meta-agent combines prior successful skills to create new skill recipes, while a base-level context engineer executes the recipe and updates context from rollout feedback.

interaction.content:
you're going to have to take me through all this - don't understand

An MCE skill defines a context function and maps an input to context, where:

static components are prompts, knowledge bases, code libraries. dynamic operators are search, selection, filtering, formatting. The bi-level optimization is to find the best context given skill on the training data, while the outer loop finds the optimal skill that provides the best performance on the validation set. The skill database tracks the history of previous skills, context functions and eval metrics. A meta-level agent performs agentic crossover over prior skills to create a new skill given a task. Then a base-level context engineer executes the skill and learns the context function from rollout feedback, guided by the current skill.

Think of MCE as optimizing the recipe for building context, not just optimizing one context note. In ACE, the system improves a playbook of useful context bullets. In MCE, the system asks a higher-level question: what is the best method for creating and updating that playbook? A skill is the recipe. It says: for this kind of task, use these resources, search these places, select this kind of evidence, format it this way, and update memory like this. Static components are the ingredients that do not change much: prompts, knowledge bases, code libraries. Dynamic operators are the actions the recipe performs: search, select, filter, format. Inner loop: given one recipe, find the best context it can produce on training examples. Outer loop: compare recipes on validation examples and pick/evolve the recipe that generalizes best. Skill database: memory of old recipes, contexts, and scores. Crossover: combine useful pieces of previous recipes to create a new recipe. Base-level context engineer: runs the recipe and updates context from trial-and-error feedback. One sentence: ACE improves the context; MCE tries to improve the method that improves the context. Check-back: in this setup, is the 'skill' the actual context shown to the model, or the recipe for constructing that context?

title: Why use markdown files instead of a database for agent memory?
marker: Context Engineering / ACE and MCE memory storage
note: User asked whether ACE/MCE are basically agents appending external markdown memory, and whether a structured database would help or be preferable.
synthesis: Yes, the intuition is right: ACE-style systems keep external memory/context outside the prompt, often as structured text or files. But databases are also used in agent memory systems, especially vector databases, SQL tables, key-value stores, and graph databases. Files are common in research harnesses because they are simple, inspectable, versionable, easy for coding agents to edit, and flexible while the memory schema is still evolving. Databases help when memory needs scale, querying, relationships, permissions, concurrency, or typed structure. The tradeoff is flexibility and agent-readability versus stricter schema and retrieval power.

interaction.content:
The fact that ACE learns insights from rollouts helps us move toward self-managed memory, but the update rules and the overall workflow are still handcrafted. To move toward a more self-improving loop, Meta Context Engineering (MCE; [Ye et al. 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.21557)

Okay, this is where my thinking is going with this, is basically just trying to have the agent write to and append an external corpus of memory, which I'm assuming here is just in markdown file format. And now they're talking about a different one. So the ACE format is that. And then metacontext engineering, I guess. But why don't people use the database here? Or are some of them using a database? Is there any case to be made for why the agent isn't reading from and writing to a structured database as opposed to markdown file? Wouldn't this help?

Yes, your intuition is good. ACE is basically moving useful lessons out of the transient prompt into an external, maintained memory/context artifact. In the paper framing, that artifact is often file-like or markdown-like because it is easy for an agent to read, edit, diff, version, and inspect. But people absolutely do use databases for agent memory: vector DBs for semantic retrieval, SQL tables for structured facts/events, key-value stores for simple state, and graph DBs for relationships between concepts. The reason markdown/files show up a lot in harness research is pragmatic: files are schema-light, human-readable, git-diffable, easy for coding agents to manipulate, and flexible when you do not yet know the right memory schema. A database helps when you need scale, precise queries, relationships, permissions, concurrency, deduplication, or typed fields. The tradeoff is that a database forces you to design the schema and retrieval API. That can help, but it can also make early experimentation brittle. So the clean mental model: markdown/files are good for evolving playbooks and inspectable agent state; databases are good when memory becomes large, relational, multi-user, or query-heavy. Check-back: if the agent is still discovering what kinds of lessons matter, would you start with a rigid schema, or a flexible file/playbook that can later be migrated into a database?

title: What is a practical example of optimizing deeper harness machinery?
marker: Harness Optimization / surface text to deeper system machinery
note: User asked for a more practical example of the optimization target moving from surface text to deeper system machinery.
synthesis: A practical example is improving a coding agent. At the surface level, you tune the prompt wording. Deeper, you change what context it receives, then the workflow it follows, then the harness code that manages tools, tests, permissions, memory, and retries. At the deepest level, you improve the optimizer that automatically searches over prompts, contexts, workflows, and harness code. The same goal shifts from editing instructions to editing the machinery that produces better behavior.

interaction.content:
can we just come back to this:

the optimization target moves from surface text to deeper system machinery.

give me more practical example of this

Practical example: imagine you want a coding agent to fix bugs more reliably. Surface text: change the prompt from 'fix the bug' to 'inspect the failing test, identify root cause, make a minimal change, run tests.' Structured context: change what the agent sees: include the failing test output, relevant files, recent diffs, repo conventions, and known gotchas. Workflow: change the loop: reproduce failure -> inspect code -> patch -> run focused test -> run broader test -> summarize. Harness code: change the actual system: automatically detect test commands, capture logs, restrict risky shell commands, save intermediate notes, retry on flaky failures, require approval for destructive actions. Optimizer code: change the system that searches for better versions of all the above: it tries different prompts/workflows/tool policies, scores them on benchmark tasks, and keeps the best variants. So 'deeper machinery' means you stop only asking 'what words should I put in the prompt?' and start asking 'what process, tools, memory, checks, and improvement loop should surround the model?' Check-back: in the bug-fixing example, which change is deeper: telling the model 'be careful', or changing the harness so it must run tests and inspect failures before declaring success?

title: What is Agentic Context Engineering in one sentence?
note: User asked for a TLDR of Agentic Context Engineering (ACE) from the context engineering section.
synthesis: ACE treats context as a structured, evolving playbook rather than a long prompt. The agent runs tasks, reflects on what worked or failed, and updates a set of durable context bullets. Its generator uses the current playbook, its reflector extracts lessons from rollouts, and its curator merges those lessons into organized context entries. The goal is to improve future performance by making context memory cleaner, more reusable, and less likely to collapse into an overloaded prompt.

interaction.content:
give me the tldr on this:

Agentic Context Engineering (ACE; [Zhang et al. 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04618))

TLDR: ACE is a way for an agent to maintain and improve its own working context over time. Instead of dumping every past message, tool result, and lesson into one giant prompt, ACE keeps a structured playbook of useful context bullets. It has three roles: Generator: tries tasks using the current context. Reflector: looks at successes/failures and extracts lessons. Curator: updates the context playbook by adding, refining, or deduplicating useful bullets. The point is: the agent learns what context helps it perform better, and stores that context in a cleaner, reusable form. Grounding: it is like replacing a messy notebook with a maintained checklist/playbook that gets improved after each attempt. Check-back: is ACE mainly about changing the model's weights, or about improving what context the model is given before future attempts?

title: What does the progression from prompts to optimizer code mean?
note: User asked what Weng means by optimizing prompts, structured context, workflow, harness code, and optimizer code as models become more powerful.
synthesis: Weng is describing a progression in what the AI system can improve. Early systems optimize simple instructions or prompts. More advanced systems optimize structured context and workflows: what information is shown and what steps are followed. Stronger systems can edit the harness code itself: the tools, loops, memory, and evaluation logic. The most advanced version optimizes the optimizer code: the machinery that searches for and improves future harnesses. The target moves from small surface text to deeper system machinery.

interaction.content:
don't really understand this >

The progression in the object being optimized in the harness system is roughly: instruction [prompts](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-03-15-prompt-engineering/) → structured context → workflow → harness code → optimizer code. As the model becomes more intelligent and powerful, we move toward more complex targets and generic methods.

She is saying: as models get stronger, we stop only tuning the words we give them, and start tuning deeper parts of the system. Think of it as levels. Level 1, instruction prompts: change the wording, like 'think step by step' or 'be concise.' Level 2, structured context: change what information the model sees and how it is organized, like summaries, examples, rules, memory, retrieved docs. Level 3, workflow: change the process the model follows, like plan -> act -> test -> revise. Level 4, harness code: change the actual software wrapper: tool APIs, memory system, permission logic, eval code, retry behavior. Level 5, optimizer code: change the code that searches for better prompts/workflows/harnesses in the first place. The key idea: weak models can only help with simple surface changes. Stronger models can modify more abstract machinery. Check-back: if an agent changes the wording of its instructions, which level is that? If it changes the loop from 'answer once' to 'write code, run tests, fix failures,' which level is that?

title: What is Weng's claim about harnesses versus core intelligence?
marker: Harness Layer vs Core Intelligence? / claim
note: User asked what Weng's claim is in the Harness Layer vs Core Intelligence section.
synthesis: Weng's claim is that near-term RSI is unlikely to come mainly from a model directly rewriting its own weights. A more practical path is improving the harness: the workflows, tools, memory, context, evaluations, and runtime around the model. Better harnesses can make the same model more useful, can enable auto-research loops that produce better future models, and smarter models can then simplify and improve harnesses in return.

interaction.content: what is her claim?

Her claim is: near-term recursive self-improvement probably will not look like a model directly rewriting its own brain. It will more likely look like better systems around the model making AI research and agent work faster. In her terms: harness engineering becomes a practical path to RSI. Better harnesses give models better tools, workflows, memory, context, evaluation, and feedback loops. Those systems help produce better research, better agents, and eventually better models. Then better models can improve the harnesses further. So the loop is: better harness -> better AI work -> better models/systems -> even better harness. Check-back: is she claiming the harness replaces model intelligence, or that harnesses amplify and organize model intelligence so the whole system improves faster?

title: What does harness layer versus core intelligence mean?
note: User asked what the section title Harness Layer vs Core Intelligence means.
synthesis: The phrase asks where an AI system's improved capability is coming from. Core intelligence means the base model itself: its trained weights, reasoning ability, knowledge, and general skill. The harness layer means the system wrapped around the model: tools, workflow loops, memory, context management, permissions, evals, and execution environment. Weng is asking whether progress comes from smarter models, better harnesses, or an interaction where each improves and amplifies the other.

interaction.content:
what is meant here?

Harness Layer vs Core Intelligence?

This section is asking: when an AI system looks more capable, is that because the model itself got smarter, or because the system around the model got better? Core intelligence = the base model's actual learned capability: reasoning, knowledge, coding ability, planning, judgment, all stored in the weights. Harness layer = the wrapper around the model: tools, workflow loops, memory, context selection, file access, tests, permissions, subagents, evals, and product/runtime design. Example: same model, weak harness: it gives one answer and forgets context. Same model, strong harness: it can edit files, run tests, remember logs, ask for permission, retry, and coordinate subtasks. The model did not change, but the total system became much more useful. Weng's question is not either/or. She is asking how much near-term progress comes from better harnesses versus better base models, and how they reinforce each other. Check-back: if GPT-5 stays the same but Codex gets better tools, memory, and eval loops, did core intelligence improve, or did the harness layer improve?

title: What are code diffs and paper summaries as agent memory?
marker: Pattern 2: File System as Persistent Memory / code diffs and paper summaries
note: User asked how code diffs are commonly stored and what paper summaries means in the file-memory examples.
synthesis: Code diffs are commonly stored as git commits, patch/diff files, pull requests, or logs from commands like git diff and git show. They preserve what changed, where it changed, and sometimes why. Paper summaries are short structured notes about research papers: the problem, core idea, method, results, limitations, and relevance to the current task. In a harness, both are durable artifacts the model can re-read instead of keeping every detail in context.

interaction.content:
what are common ways to store code diffs? 

and 'paper summarries' meaning what?

Common ways to store code diffs: git commits, which are the normal durable record of changes; patch files, such as `fix-bug.patch`, which are portable diff files; pull requests, which store the diff plus discussion and review; `git diff` logs saved to files, useful during experiments; and structured change notes, like `changed X because Y, tests Z passed`. A code diff means: the before/after changes to code, usually line-by-line. Paper summaries means concise notes about papers the agent has read. Not a full copy of the paper. More like: what problem does this paper solve, what is the core method, what did it find, what are the limitations, and why does it matter for this task? In long research workflows, the agent might read 30 papers. It should not keep all paper text in context; it should save useful summaries and pull them back when needed. Check-back: if an agent reads a paper about a new evaluation method, should it save the whole PDF in prompt context, or save a compact summary with the key method, findings, and relevance?

title: Why store agent memory in files instead of context?
marker: Pattern 2: File System as Persistent Memory
note: User asked whether the listed artifacts are examples of things that should be stored in memory.
synthesis: Yes: Weng is listing examples of durable state that should live outside the model's immediate context. The point is not just 'store memories'; it is 'do not make the chat/context window carry the whole long-running task.' Experiment logs, code diffs, paper summaries, error traces, and rollout histories can become too large, so the harness saves them in files and retrieves the relevant pieces when needed.

interaction.content:
these are just a quick mention of things that should be stored in 'memory' right?

A recurring pattern in long-horizon agent systems is simple control over rich states and artifacts. A harness should not carry the entire workflow and all logs in context; instead, it should keep durable state in files. In long-horizon agentic rollout, artifacts such as experiment logs, code diffs, paper summaries, error traces, and past rollout trajectories often grow much longer than the context window that the model has trained for.

Yes, but with one sharper distinction: she means file-backed memory, not memory as 'stuff currently in the prompt.' Those examples, experiment logs, code diffs, paper summaries, error traces, and past trajectories, are the durable record of a long task. They should be stored somewhere stable, usually files, because they can get too large for the model's context window. The model can later read the relevant pieces back in. So the pattern is: context = short-term working memory; files = long-term task memory/artifact store. Check-back: if an agent runs 40 experiments, should every log stay inside the prompt forever, or should the harness save logs to files and pull in only the relevant parts when needed?

title: What are the three harness design patterns?
marker: Harness Design Patterns / three patterns TLDR
note: User asked for a TLDR of workflow automation, file system as persistent memory, and sub-agent/backend jobs.
synthesis: The three patterns are: workflow automation, where the harness gives the model a repeatable loop such as plan, act, observe, test, and improve; file system as persistent memory, where long-running state, logs, artifacts, and decisions live in files instead of fragile chat context; and sub-agent/backend jobs, where the harness can run parallel workers or background processes and later merge their results. Together they make an AI system more like a durable runtime than a one-shot prompt.

interaction.content:
what are these three patterns, tldr

[Pattern 1: Workflow Automation](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2026-07-04-harness/#pattern-1-workflow-automation)
[Pattern 2: File System as Persistent Memory](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2026-07-04-harness/#pattern-2-file-system-as-persistent-memory)
[Pattern 3: Sub-agent and Backend Jobs](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2026-07-04-harness/#pattern-3-sub-agent-and-backend-jobs)

TLDR: these are three ways to make an AI agent durable and useful beyond a single prompt. Pattern 1, workflow automation: give the model a loop. It does not just answer once; it plans, acts, observes results, tests, fixes, and repeats. Pattern 2, file system as persistent memory: do not keep everything in the chat context. Save plans, logs, outputs, notes, code, errors, and artifacts in files so the agent can recover and continue. Pattern 3, sub-agent and backend jobs: let the harness run multiple workers or long-running tasks in parallel, then inspect their outputs and merge the useful results. Grounding example: Codex uses all three. It follows a work loop, saves/reads files, and can run commands or delegate parallel work. Check-back: which pattern solves the problem of the model forgetting long task history: workflow loops, files as memory, or subagents?

title: What is the high-level map of the harness engineering article?
note: User asked for a TLDR of the full article outline before continuing, to ground the high-level structure.
synthesis: The article argues that near-term recursive self-improvement may happen less through models rewriting their own weights and more through better harnesses: the runtime systems around models. First it explains basic harness design patterns: workflow loops, files as durable memory, subagents and background jobs, with coding agents as the concrete case. Then it asks whether harnesses are separate from core intelligence or a path to amplify it. The middle sections cover optimizing harnesses: context engineering, workflow design, self-improving harnesses, evolutionary search, and possibly joint optimization with model weights. The ending explains hard problems: weak evaluators, memory lifecycle, negative results, diversity collapse, reward hacking, long-term maintainability, and the role of humans.

TLDR: the article is about how AI systems can get more capable by improving the machinery around the model, not only by making the model weights smarter. The first part gives the basic patterns: automate a loop, save state in files, run subagents/background jobs, and look at coding agents as the clearest example. The middle asks the big question: how much of future AI improvement comes from better harnesses versus better core models? Then it surveys ways to optimize the harness itself: better context management, better workflows, harnesses that edit themselves, evolutionary search over programs/agents, and eventually combining harness changes with model-weight updates. title: What does the operating system analogy mean for harnesses?
marker: Harness Design Patterns / operating system analogy
note: User asked what exactly an operating system means in Weng's analogy between OSes and harnesses.
synthesis: An operating system is the software layer that sits between applications and the machine's messy details: files, memory, processes, devices, permissions, and scheduling. The analogy is that a harness sits between the model and the messy external world: tools, context, memory, workflows, permissions, evaluation, artifacts, and execution. Like an OS, a good harness hides complicated coordination behind a simpler interface.

interaction.content:
what exactly is an operating system in the example given here?

The design should be deliberately simple and generic to enable generalization, likely with reference to existing software engineering practices to benefit from prertaining knowlege. There is also a strong analogy between operating systems and harnesses. Similar to an OS, a harness should encapsulate complicated logic while keeping the interface simple. Meanwhile, configs, tool interfaces and other protocols may gradually become standardized across the industry.

An operating system is the layer that lets programs use a computer without each program having to manage all the messy details itself. For example, an app does not directly control the disk, RAM, keyboard, network card, permissions, and process scheduling from scratch. It asks the OS through simpler interfaces: read this file, start this process, allocate memory, send this packet, check this permission. Weng is saying a harness should do something similar for a model. The model should not have to manually manage every low-level detail of tool calls, memory, context, retries, permissions, evals, and artifacts. The harness wraps that complexity and gives the model a simpler way to observe, act, remember, and check itself. So in the analogy: OS sits between app and hardware; harness sits between model and real-world task environment. Check-back: in Codex, when I call a shell command or edit a file, am I directly being the base model doing raw computer control, or am I using harness/tool interfaces that simplify and constrain what I can do?

title: What makes harness engineering more than an early agent framework?
marker: Harness Design Patterns / agent framework vs harness engineering
note: User asked whether agent = LLM + memory + tools + planning + action is early software engineering, and whether workflow design, evals, permissions, and persistent state are what make it harness engineering.
synthesis: The early agent formula names the ingredients of an agent: model, memory, tools, planning, and action. Harness engineering is more like designing the runtime system that governs those ingredients in production: what loop the model follows, how success is evaluated, what actions are permitted, what state persists outside context, and how the system observes, checks, retries, and improves. The shift is from assembling agent components to engineering the operating environment around the model.

interaction.content:
Compared with [early agent frameworks](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-06-23-agent/), “agent = LLM + memory + tools + planning + action”, harnesses engineering additionally include workflow design (e.g. loop engineering), evaluation, permission controls, and persistent state management. It is no longer only prompt templates, but closer to runtime and software system design: how the model observes, acts, memorizes, checks itself, and improves.

is the agent = llm + memory + tools + planning + action > is this just early software engineering? > 

but she is saying these extra things make it harness eng?

workflow design (e.g. loop engineering), evaluation, permission controls, and persistent state management.

Yes, you have it. The early formula is more like an ingredient list: LLM + memory + tools + planning + action. It says what pieces an agent has. Harness engineering is more like designing the runtime around those pieces: what loop does the agent follow, when does it stop, how does it check its work, what is it allowed to do, where does durable state live, how does it recover after failure, and how does it improve next time? So it is not exactly just early software engineering. Early agent frameworks were already software, but often framed as plugging capabilities into an LLM. Weng is saying mature harness work looks more like serious systems engineering: control loops, evals, permissions, state, observability, and product/runtime behavior. Check-back: if we give a model a calculator tool, that fits the early agent formula. If we design when it must use the calculator, how to verify the result, what logs to save, and when to ask permission, which layer are we now engineering?

title: How is harness engineering different from other RSI routes?
note: User asked whether Weng means harness engineering is one route to RSI while self-play, synthetic data, test-time training, and continual learning are other routes.
synthesis: Weng is separating different routes toward recursive self-improvement. Harness engineering improves the system around the model: tools, workflows, memory, evaluation, and execution loops. Self-play, synthetic data, test-time training, and continual learning are more model/training-centered routes: they improve the model through new data, practice, adaptation, or ongoing learning. The post focuses on harness engineering, while acknowledging those other routes also fit the broader RSI vision.

interaction.content:
I don't fully understand this:

This one post will focus on research around harness engineering and how it contributes to RSI. Much recent work on auto-research, self-improving agents, and evolutionary program search can be organized around this question. Other work on model self-play, synthetic data, test-time training and a broader theme of continual learning also matches the RSI vision (e.g. [Yuan et al. 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.10020), [Chen et al. 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.01335)), [Zhao et al. 2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.03335), [Choi et al. 2026](https://openreview.net/forum?id=lTbBFAoPSA))) but they will not be the focus of this post.

is 
She's saying that harness engineering is one way to achieve rsi - whereas those other methods - sef-play, continual learning etc? are another?

Yes, that's basically right. She's carving the RSI landscape into routes. Harness engineering is one route: improve the system around the model, such as tools, workflows, memory, evals, permissions, and execution loops. That can make the overall AI system better without changing the model weights. The other methods are also RSI-flavored, but they work closer to the model/training side: self-play means the model practices against itself or generated problems; synthetic data means better models help create training data for future models; test-time training means the model adapts while solving a task; continual learning means the model keeps learning over time instead of being fixed after training. So her scope is: this post is about the outside-the-model route, not the model-training routes. Check-back: if an AI coding agent gets better because we add better tools and memory, is that harness engineering or model training? If it gets better because its weights are updated from new practice data, which bucket is that?

title: Is the deployment system the same thing as the harness?
note: User asked whether Weng's phrase deployment system means the harness or whether the harness is one part of it.
synthesis: The deployment system is the broader real-world layer that turns a base model into a usable product or agent. A harness is one important part of that layer: it controls how the model is prompted, given tools, looped through tasks, managed with context, checked, and connected to artifacts. The deployment system also includes serving infrastructure, product UI, permissions, monitoring, evaluation, logging, routing, safety systems, and user workflows.

interaction.content:
she says deployment system?

“deployment system”

is this the harness - or harness is just one part?

The harness is one major part of the deployment system, but not the whole thing. Think of three layers: base model = the trained weights; harness = the runtime wrapper that decides prompts, tools, planning loops, context, memory, artifacts, checks, and retries; deployment system = the whole setup that makes the model useful in the world, including the harness plus serving infrastructure, product UI, permissions, logging, evals, monitoring, safety controls, routing, and human workflows. In this article, Weng is zooming in on the harness because it is the part of deployment that can make a model much more capable without changing the model weights. Check-back: if Codex uses GPT-5, a terminal, file edits, git, tests, permissions, and this Learning Lab memory, which part is the base model and which parts are harness/deployment around it?

title: What does accelerated AI research in frontier labs imply?
marker: Introduction / quote: speed of research development
note: User asked what Lilian Weng is implying by saying AI research development has drastically accelerated in frontier labs.
synthesis: The sentence implies that frontier AI labs may already be experiencing a weak form of recursive self-improvement: AI tools and agents help researchers work faster, which helps build better AI systems, which then further accelerate research. The claim is not that models are autonomously rewriting their own weights; it is that the research-and-deployment pipeline around models is speeding up because AI is becoming part of the work loop.

interaction.content:
what is being implied here?

The speed of research development in AI has been shown to drastically accelerated in frontier labs ([Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/institute/recursive-self-improvement); [OpenAI](https://openai.com/index/how-agents-are-transforming-work/)).

She is implying a weak, practical version of recursive self-improvement may already be happening inside frontier labs. Not: "the model wakes up and rewrites itself." More like: AI systems help researchers code, search literature, run experiments, analyze failures, write evals, and build better tools. That speeds up AI research. Faster AI research produces better models and better harnesses. Those better systems then make the next round of research faster again. So the loop is: AI tools improve the AI lab's work process -> the lab builds better AI -> better AI further improves the work process. Check-back: in this sentence, is the acceleration coming mainly from the model directly editing its own weights, or from AI being inserted into the human research-and-engineering workflow?
